followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I slightly raise the score relative to cycle 1 because the other reviewers correctly highlighted concrete support for ordinary WKB/time-domain agreement and for dilaton shifts being larger than adjacent WKB-order differences. I do not adopt the more positive routine verdicts because the central near-axis quasi-resonance claim still depends on extrapolated WKB, underspecified massive boundary conditions, and sparse validation.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Treating the quasi-resonance claim as well supported is too strong; the evidence for actual zero-damping critical values remains extrapolative and method-limited.

The suggestion that Zinhailo 2019 arXiv:1909.12664 may already be a systematic massive scalar EMD analysis appears unfounded; the arXiv title and abstract identify it as massless Dirac perturbations in EDGB/EW gravity.

The objection that the metric ansatz itself may become invalid before extremality is weak as stated; the better concern is WKB validity, near-horizon behavior, and branch tracking near extremal charge.
The HTML rendering failure should not by itself be counted as proof that the published PDF is unclear or mathematically wrong, though it does limit this review's auditability.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The low-l sector is a genuine weak point: WKB is least reliable there, no low-l time-domain benchmark is shown, and the reported purely real entry needs verification.
The dilaton-shift claim is better supported than I gave it credit for in cycle 1, at least as a statement about the reported WKB dataset and adjacent-order mismatch.
The near-extremal alpha=sqrt(3) branch behaving as highly damped and nearly mu-independent is a real caveat to a uniform quasi-resonance narrative.
The unreadable equations and tables in the supplied HTML bundle are a serious auditability problem, even if likely caused by rendering rather than by the author.

main_claims.claim: Massive scalar QNM spectra for EMD black holes are computed across several charges and dilaton couplings using WKB16/WKB14 with Pade improvement and time-domain evolution.
main_claims.notes: The methodology is standard and the prose reports broad parameter scans, but the supplied HTML bundle loses most equations and table entries. Only two explicit time-domain benchmarks are described, so the full numerical dataset is not independently auditable from the provided source.

main_claims.claim: Increasing scalar mass suppresses damping and indicates an approach to quasi-resonant long-lived oscillations for multiple dilaton couplings.
main_claims.notes: The qualitative damping trend is plausible and supported by the authors' reported tables/figures. The stronger statement that modes become arbitrarily long-lived at critical masses relies on smooth extrapolation toward zero damping and on WKB in a near-real-axis regime where the paper itself says precision is poor.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract; Section IV around Figures 5-6; Conclusions

main_claims.claim: Dilaton-induced shifts in the spectra are larger than the estimated numerical uncertainty.
main_claims.notes: Other reviewers are right that the paper gives concrete examples where the reported dilaton shift is much larger than WKB14-WKB16 mismatch. However, adjacent-order agreement is not a complete systematic uncertainty model near extra turning points, low multipoles, or threshold massive boundary conditions.

main_claims.claim: Lower-multipole sectors contain additional near-resonant or even purely real entries.
main_claims.notes: This is one of the more delicate claims. The paper notes poorer WKB behavior in low multipoles, and no time-domain check is shown for the reported purely real entry; it may be a numerical artifact or a different spectral object.
main_claims.paper_location: Section IV paragraph after Figure 6; Tables III-V

main_claims.claim: QNM-greybody correspondence can estimate greybody factors from the computed frequencies.
main_claims.notes: This is a standard ancillary observation and the paper itself flags limitations away from the eikonal/single-barrier regime. It is not a central new contribution.
main_claims.paper_location: End of Section IV; Eq. (18) discussion

missing_assumptions.assumption: A precise massive outgoing-wave branch convention at infinity, including behavior when omega is near the mass threshold.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The central quasi-resonance interpretation depends on whether the modes are true QNMs, quasi-bound states, or threshold resonances.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A stated criterion for when each parameter point has a dominant barrier maximum suitable for WKB.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section III after the WKB formula; Tables I-V
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The authors acknowledge additional turning points and possible loss of a maximum, but do not identify which table entries are safe, marginal, or excluded.

missing_assumptions.assumption: An uncertainty model beyond adjacent WKB-order mismatch.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Adjacent-order agreement can miss systematic errors from boundary conditions, low multipoles, branch changes, and near-axis extrapolation.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Detailed time-domain extraction choices: grid spacing, convergence, fitting window, and Prony stability tests.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Massive-field late-time oscillatory tails can contaminate ringdown fits, especially as damping becomes very small.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A mode-tracking convention across charge, mass, and dilaton coupling.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section IV discussion of Tables I-V and the near-extremal alpha=sqrt(3) behavior
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The highly damped, almost mu-independent near-extremal branch may not be continuously comparable to the weakly damped branches used in the quasi-resonance narrative.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the PDF/source formulas and Tables I-V specify the massive-field potential, boundary conditions, and numerical values correctly despite the HTML formula/table loss?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The supplied bundle omits most mathematical content and table entries, so I cannot independently verify the exact equations or data.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the near-resonant modes true outgoing QNMs rather than quasi-bound states or threshold resonances?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires checking the actual asymptotic branch convention and numerical implementation, not just the prose description.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does a convergent near-axis method such as Leaver/Frobenius or direct integration reproduce the extrapolated zero-damping critical masses?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper explicitly leaves this as future work, and WKB trends alone do not settle it.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the reported purely real low-multipole entry reproduced outside WKB?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: No time-domain or independent frequency-domain check for that parameter point is provided in the bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the branches being compared continuously tracked across mu, Q, and alpha, especially near the high-charge alpha=sqrt(3) regime?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper's prose reports branch behavior but does not provide enough numerical diagnostics to verify mode identity across the scan.

novelty_assessment: The novelty remains modest. The paper appears to fill a narrow parameter-space gap for massive scalar perturbations of static charged EMD black holes, but quasi-resonances of massive fields and the WKB-Pade plus time-domain workflow are well established. I reject the specific novelty objection that Zinhailo 2019 arXiv:1909.12664 already covers massive scalar EMD perturbations: that paper is on a massless Dirac field in Einstein-dilaton-Gauss-Bonnet and Einstein-Weyl gravities. Still, related dilaton and massive-field QNM literature makes this a useful cataloging contribution rather than a field-shaping result.
score_rationale: The ordinary damping trends and dilaton-dependent shifts look plausible and are likely useful to the subfield, so I am not treating the paper as likely wrong. But the central claim of robust quasi-resonances at critical masses is not established with a method reliable at the real axis, and key boundary-condition and WKB-validity assumptions are underspecified. The result is a contested, potentially publishable parameter study only after human checks or stronger near-axis validation, not a technically settled result.
summary: The paper is an incremental numerical study of massive scalar QNMs of charged Einstein-Maxwell-dilaton black holes using high-order WKB-Pade estimates and two time-domain Prony checks. I am slightly more positive than in cycle 1 about the ordinary, non-near-axis trends: the reviewers correctly emphasized that the paper gives concrete WKB/time-domain agreement examples and a plausible dilaton-dependent shift larger than adjacent-order WKB differences. I remain unconvinced that the strongest claim, true quasi-resonances with arbitrarily small damping at critical masses, is established rather than extrapolated from a method whose reliability is explicitly weakest in that regime.

technical_objections.evidence: For a massive scalar, the asymptotic wave number involves a branch such as sqrt(omega^2-mu^2). Near Re(omega) close to the scalar mass and Im(omega) close to zero, the branch choice distinguishes outgoing QNMs, quasi-bound states, and threshold resonances. The supplied text only states outgoing/ingoing conditions generically.
technical_objections.objection: The massive-field boundary condition at infinity is not specified with enough precision for the near-threshold regime.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III, Eq. (13); Section IV quasi-resonance discussion around Figure 6

technical_objections.evidence: The paper says dotted curves are smooth extrapolations toward zero damping and that WKB is not sufficiently reliable extremely close to the real axis, but then calls the approach to arbitrarily long-lived modes unambiguous. A dedicated near-axis method is deferred to future work.
technical_objections.objection: The main quasi-resonance conclusion is overclaimed relative to the demonstrated numerical regime.
technical_objections.paper_location: Figure 6 caption and surrounding text; Conclusions

technical_objections.evidence: The authors acknowledge that massive-field potentials may develop extra turning points or lose a local maximum. Neighboring high-order WKB agreement does not by itself validate the boundary-condition treatment, peak dominance, or low-l behavior; no entry-by-entry exclusion criterion is given.
technical_objections.objection: WKB14-WKB16 agreement is used too strongly as an error estimate in regimes where systematic WKB failure is plausible.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III WKB-method discussion; Section IV first paragraph

technical_objections.evidence: Only two Prony comparisons are reported, apparently in well-behaved sectors. The most delicate claims involve low multipoles, near-zero damping, high charge, and branch changes, but those points are not independently checked by time evolution or a convergent frequency-domain method.
technical_objections.objection: Time-domain validation is too sparse for the strongest parameter-space and near-resonant claims.
technical_objections.paper_location: Figures 3-4; final paragraph of Section IV

technical_objections.evidence: For a non-extremal black-hole QNM with outgoing boundary conditions, exactly zero damping would be exceptional. Since low-l WKB is less reliable and the actual table values are not visible in the supplied bundle, this entry needs separate verification.
technical_objections.objection: The reported purely real low-multipole entry is suspicious without independent confirmation.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section IV paragraph discussing Table III low-l behavior

technical_objections.evidence: Most formulas and table cells appear as blank placeholders in the provided source. This may be a LaTeXML/rendering artifact rather than an author-side mathematical error, but it prevents checking the action normalization, potential, WKB formula, boundary conditions, and numerical values from the bundle.
technical_objections.objection: The supplied HTML bundle has severe auditability failures for equations and numerical tables.
technical_objections.paper_location: Equations (1)-(18); Tables I-V in the provided arXiv HTML text
